name: ASUS ROG Swift PG279QM 27-Inch 1440p 240Hz Gaming Monitor

specs.Stand: Full ergonomic adjustment (tilt, swivel, pivot, height)

whatWorks: The combination of Fast IPS technology and 240Hz refresh rate is the sweet spot for competitive gaming in 2024. You get the color accuracy and wide viewing angles of IPS alongside the 1ms GTG response time that keeps ghosting invisible even in fast-paced FPS games. Most 240Hz IPS monitors make you choose between color or speed — the PG279QM genuinely delivers both.

1440p at 27 inches hits the ideal pixel density for gaming without the GPU demand of 4K. At 109 PPI, individual pixels are invisible at normal viewing distances, text is sharp, and game worlds look detailed without requiring a high-end GPU to maintain 240 frames per second. It is the resolution that makes the most sense for competitive play in 2024.

NVIDIA G-Sync compatibility, combined with VESA Adaptive-Sync, means the monitor eliminates screen tearing across a wide frame rate range without forcing you into a G-Sync-specific GPU. The panel syncs smoothly from 48Hz to 270Hz, which means even on older hardware or in GPU-heavy scenes, the experience remains tear-free.

The ergonomic stand is genuinely good — height adjustment, swivel, tilt, and 90-degree pivot for portrait mode are all included. The 4-port USB-A 3.0 hub built into the monitor is a practical bonus that desktop setups benefit from immediately, reducing the cable management burden on your PC's rear ports.
worthKnowing: DisplayHDR 400 certification is the entry-level HDR tier and should not be treated as a reason to buy this monitor. The panel's peak brightness of 400 nits does not deliver the high-contrast HDR experience you see on OLED or high-end VA panels. Think of this as an excellent SDR gaming monitor that has passable HDR rather than a true HDR display.

Reaching the 240Hz native refresh rate requires a DisplayPort 1.4 connection — HDMI 2.0 caps out at 144Hz due to bandwidth limitations. Make sure your GPU has a DisplayPort output and use the included DisplayPort cable for the full 240Hz experience. The overclock to 270Hz requires enabling it in the OSD settings.

At $799 CAD, this monitor is positioned at the premium end of the 1440p 240Hz market. Competitors like the LG 27GP850-B offer similar performance at $499 CAD with Nano IPS technology. The PG279QM justifies the premium through better build quality, the 270Hz overclock option, and the more comprehensive USB hub — but the value gap is real.

The monitor uses a VA-like backlight system that can exhibit some blooming in very dark scenes with bright elements. It is not pronounced and most gaming content is unlikely to expose it, but users who watch a lot of dark HDR content on a PC monitor may prefer a dedicated dark-room panel.

commonComplaints.title: Premium price for features available cheaper
commonComplaints.body: At $799 CAD, several buyers noted that monitors like the LG 27GP850-B at $499 deliver comparable gaming performance. The ASUS ROG premium buys you incremental improvements in build and the 270Hz overclock, but the value math is difficult to defend for budget-conscious buyers.

commonComplaints.title: HDR 400 is underwhelming
commonComplaints.body: Multiple buyers who purchased expecting a meaningful HDR experience were disappointed. DisplayHDR 400 is a checkbox certification — the brightness and contrast ratio are not sufficient to create the HDR pop that TVs and OLED monitors deliver.

commonComplaints.title: HDMI bandwidth limits refresh rate
commonComplaints.body: Console users who connected via HDMI discovered the monitor caps at 144Hz over HDMI 2.0. The 240Hz rate requires DisplayPort 1.4, which PS5 and Xbox Series X do not output natively. This monitor is primarily optimized for PC gaming.

commonComplaints.title: Stand wobble at some heights
commonComplaints.body: A small number of reviewers reported that the stand has slight wobble at its highest extension, which can be distracting during intense gaming sessions where the desk vibrates. The wobble is minor but present, and aftermarket VESA mounts eliminate it entirely.

verdict: The ASUS ROG Swift PG279QM is among the best 27-inch 1440p gaming monitors available, combining Fast IPS color accuracy with 240Hz competitive-grade speed in a premium package. The HDR implementation is a checkbox feature rather than a genuine capability, and the price premium over competitors is real — but for PC gamers who want the best in class for competitive play without moving to 4K, it delivers confidently.

realBuyers.quote: The difference between 144Hz and 240Hz was not subtle — gaming feels physically smoother. Mouse tracking in particular feels more precise and responsive. I can not imagine going back.
Colors are gorgeous for an IPS panel. My previous TN monitor looked washed out by comparison. I can actually enjoy single-player games now instead of just tolerating the display.
G-Sync at high frame rates is seamless. I have been gaming at 200+ fps in Valorant and the experience is buttery smooth with zero tearing. The investment paid off immediately.

The USB hub built into the monitor cleaned up my desk cable situation significantly. I plugged my keyboard, mouse, and headset into the monitor instead of reaching behind the PC. Small thing, big quality-of-life improvement.

metaDescription: ASUS ROG Swift PG279QM review: 27-inch 1440p 240Hz Fast IPS gaming monitor with G-Sync. The best all-around competitive gaming display. $799 CAD on Amazon.ca.
introText: The ASUS ROG Swift PG279QM (ASIN B08XXXCJ8Q) is available on Amazon.ca for $799 CAD and delivers the combination of 27-inch Fast IPS color accuracy with a 240Hz native refresh rate that serious PC gamers have wanted for years. The panel's 1ms GTG response time and NVIDIA G-Sync compatibility make it one of the most complete competitive gaming monitors at the 1440p tier.
